title: Differential Rateband Schematic & Cost Composer for Token Attribution and Pricing UI Patterns
preview:
Background: Teams have asked for more fine-grained control and explainability over per-token billing. Current Console views provide breakdowns but none that allow operators to define or preview differential rate bands (e.g., model-specific premiums, quantization discounts, regional multipliers) and immediately see token-attribution lines and exportable line-items for billing reconciliation. This ticket scopes a design prototype: a composable 'Rateband Composer' UI pattern that sits alongside token-level trace and attribution, feeds the cost simulation engine, and produces CSV/JSON exports suitable for downstream chargeback and billing systems.

Goals: Provide a compact visual language for defining rate bands, previewing applied rates on real token traces, enable fast pivoting between aggregation dimensions (model, route, user role), and offer an exportable line-item breakdown that maps back to token spans. Deliver a Figma prototype and handoff-ready spec for engineering.
text:

Goals: Provide a compact visual language for defining rate bands, previewing applied rates on real token traces, enable fast pivoting between aggregation dimensions (model, route, user role), and offer an exportable line-item breakdown that maps back to token spans. Deliver a Figma prototype and handoff-ready spec for engineering.

title: Compact manifest differential sync and smart backoff for customer usage exports
preview:
Problem: Large tenant CSV exports create huge S3 manifests and high downstream processing cost for customers that consume scheduled exports or webhooks. Existing full-manifest deliveries repeatedly resend unchanged rows during retries and amplify transfer costs. Goal: implement a compact, differential manifest format plus a delivery policy that uses exponential backoff with granularity escalation to reduce duplicate transfer and improve delivery success rates while preserving strong ordering and idempotence for consumers.

Approach: produce delta manifests that enumerate only changed file shards (created/updated/deleted) and a small per-tenant checkpoint token. Each manifest entry will include a stable content-hash, byte-range hints, and an optional ephemeral cursor for resumable downloads. On delivery failure we progressively broaden the granularity of the next manifest (e.g., from shard-level diffs -> partition-level snapshot -> full snapshot) to improve recovery odds for flaky consumers, using jittered exponential backoff. Retries are idempotent: S3 filenames include a deterministic manifest version + hash so repeat deliveries are no-ops for consumers that check hashes. Webhook delivery uses at-least-once semantics with required ack headers; if no ack is received the manifest is retried with backoff and the manifest includes a short 'repair' hint pointing to a compact reconciliation set.

Operational decisions and trade-offs: We prioritize bandwidth and customer cost over minimal latency for large tenants. Compact manifests add a small compute overhead on export generation; to limit tail latency we will pre-compute shard checksums in the export pipeline and reuse them between runs. We will not block scheduled deliveries on full snapshot generation; instead, full snapshot generation runs asynchronously and is referenced by the manifest when available. For strict regulatory tenants we will include KMS-encrypted pointers and require server-side encryption on S3 uploads.

Telemetry and observability: emit manifest.size_bytes, manifest.delta_ratio, delivery.attempts, delivery.success_latency_ms, webhook.ack_latency_ms, and downstream-reconciliation-rate per-tenant. Add alert on per-tenant repeated escalation to full snapshots (>3 escalations in 24h).

Rollout plan: implement on a canary subset of tenants (low-volume first), validate cost savings vs CPU overhead, then expand to larger tenants with opt-in before forced migration.

Deliverables: compact manifest schema, exporter changes (generator + signer), delivery service backoff state machine, webhook ack contract update in docs, integration tests, migration job to compute initial checksums for existing snapshots.

title: Per-model quantization prescription lifecycle and shadow validation harness
preview:
Objective:
Provide a reproducible, auditable lifecycle for selecting and publishing per-model quantization prescriptions (FP8 / INT8) in the Model Catalog, plus a shadow validation harness that verifies accuracy and safety guardrails before any rollout.

Background:
We have multiple ad-hoc calibration scripts and manual checks when quantizing models. This leads to inconsistent defaults in the catalog and unexpected quality regressions for customers. We need a standard lifecycle: Local calibration -> CI gating -> Shadow serving validation -> Canary rollout -> Full publish, with clear metrics, thresholds, and rollback rules.

Proposal (high level):
1) Prescription format: standard artifact (JSON metadata + artifact link) containing: model-version, quant-mode (FP8/INT8), calibration-seed, calibration-dataset-hash, recommended-kernel-profile, expected-latency-bucket, expected-cost-estimate, quality-bands (offline delta thresholds), safety-checks triggered list, provenance links (PR/drive).
2) Calibration workflow: reproducible calibration job run in calibration CI using a curated, sampled prompt set per model (see sampling rules). Job produces: calibrated weights, per-layer scaling factors, calibration logs, and numeric metrics (PPL-delta, top-k accuracy, MRR, response-length normalized entropy).
3) Shadow validation harness: integrate with Redwood serving runtime to run shadow requests for a time-window against quantized artifact while recording: token-level divergence, softlogits-delta, output-length changes, latency percentiles, and task-specific metrics (e.g. Rouge/BLEU where applicable). No customer traffic is impacted.
4) Gates and thresholds: define default thresholds and model-specific override bands. Examples: PPL increase <= 2.5% OR absolute delta <= 0.03; hallucination signal (qa-check) false-positive rate delta <= 1.5%; token-distribution drift KL <= 0.05; 95p latency increase <= 15ms.
5) Rollout recipe: local -> PR gated CI run (must pass unit-level numeric thresholds) -> shadow validation for 72h with synthetic + replayed real traffic -> canary at 5% for 24h -> 25% for 48h -> full. Each step has automatic rollback if monitored metrics breach.

Sampling rules for calibration & validation datasets:
- For public LMs: use stratified sample across instruction types, context lengths, and LLM capabilities (summarization, code, chat, retrieval prompts).
- For customer-sensitive models: only use anonymized aggregate prompts or synthetic benchmarks.
- Minimum sample size: 2k prompts for large models (>7B), 5k for >30B, with targeted hard-case subset of 500 prompts.

Telemetry and observability requirements:
- Expose per-prescription dashboard in Console: PPL, MRR, top-1 token divergence heatmap, latency p95/p99, failure rates, and rollback triggers.
- Persist calibration artifacts to S3 with immutable keys and link in model catalog entry.
- CI job must publish numeric summary JSON to artifact store and attach to PR.

Guardrails and safe-fallbacks:
- If a prescription fails shadow validation, automatic fallback to previous published quantization or FP16 baseline.
- All published catalog entries must list a fallback chain and confidence band.
- For high-risk models (customer-facing generation), require secondary human sign-off for FP8 publication.

Integrations and artifacts:
- Model Catalog API: extension to accept prescription artifact link and metadata.
- Console UI: new 'Quantization' tab per model with recommended default and validation history.
- CI: new Jenkins/GHA job 'quant-calibrate' and 'quant-validate' that run in reproducible runtime.
- GitHub PR: add automatic comment with numerical summary and link to shadow validation run.
- Storage: s3://redwood-quant-artifacts/<model>/<version>/<prescription-id>/

Acceptance criteria (high level):
- The team can produce a prescription artifact for any single model-version via the new calibration CI job.
- Shadow validation harness integrates with runtime and records the required telemetry for at least two model families (one 7B and one 33B).
- Console shows prescription metadata and the latest validation summary for a model entry.
- CI gating prevents merge of quantized artifacts that exceed configured thresholds.

Long-term (future):
- Automated per-workload recommendation engine that proposes the default prescription (based on cost/latency constraints and historical validation results).
- Model-specific calibration seeds stored and replayable for deterministic comparisons.

title: SDK-side telemetry backpressure and prioritized span emission
preview: Problem: SDKs that emit tracing and lightweight telemetry for streaming LLM requests are contributing measurable CPU/IO overhead and amplifying tail latency under high client concurrency. Current approach emits spans/metrics synchronously or with naive unbounded buffers which leads to client-side stalls and dropped telemetry during backpressure. Goal: implement a client-side emission protocol that applies adaptive batching, prioritized span tagging, and backpressure-aware flush policies to keep SDK overhead low while preserving fidelity for latency/error analysis.
text: Problem: SDKs that emit tracing and lightweight telemetry for streaming LLM requests are contributing measurable CPU/IO overhead and amplifying tail latency under high client concurrency. Current approach emits spans/metrics synchronously or with naive unbounded buffers which leads to client-side stalls and dropped telemetry during backpressure. Goal: implement a client-side emission protocol that applies adaptive batching, prioritized span tagging, and backpressure-aware flush policies to keep SDK overhead low while preserving fidelity for latency/error analysis.

title: Orbital Pivot: Console navigation decomposition and compact workspace switcher prototype
preview: Goal: Replace the current top-heavy workspace/navigation patterns with a compact, persistent left pivot that supports fast workspace switching, per-route cost lenses, and rollout state awareness without adding cognitive load. This ticket contains the primary prototype and the decomposition of the navigation into three independent layers: 1) global workspace selector and identity surface; 2) route cluster rail (primary product areas like Models, Deployments, Dashboards, Billing); 3) contextual micro-rail for per-route actions (rollouts, traces, cost breakdowns). The prototype explores a compressed collapsed state (icon-only), focused state (expanded icons + micro-labels), and a transient overlay for quick workspace switching with fuzzy search. Key constraints: preserve token-level cost visibility in Dashboards route, support canary/cascade rollout indicators in the rail, maintain 60px touch targets for mobile breakpoints, and ensure route persistence across private/dedicated/hosted account modes.
text: Goal: Replace the current top-heavy workspace/navigation patterns with a compact, persistent left pivot that supports fast workspace switching, per-route cost lenses, and rollout state awareness without adding cognitive load. This ticket contains the primary prototype and the decomposition of the navigation into three independent layers: 1) global workspace selector and identity surface; 2) route cluster rail (primary product areas like Models, Deployments, Dashboards, Billing); 3) contextual micro-rail for per-route actions (rollouts, traces, cost breakdowns). The prototype explores a compressed collapsed state (icon-only), focused state (expanded icons + micro-labels), and a transient overlay for quick workspace switching with fuzzy search. title: Tenant-aware audit data lifecycle and cost-QoS for RBAC v2 events
preview:
Background:
Redwood needs predictable cost and query performance for audit events generated by RBAC v2 and SSO/SAML admin actions. Tenant activity patterns vary widely: some tenants generate millions of admin-audit events per day, others generate only a few. Current pipeline writes all events to a single hot index topology which drives up storage and query costs for heavy tenants and increases noise for investigations.

Goals:
- Ensure high-value, security-relevant audit events (RBAC changes, SSO admin actions, KMS operations) are retained and indexed for fast query for the configured retention window.
- Provide tenant-level QoS that limits ingestion cost for high-volume tenants while guaranteeing retention/availability for tenants with compliance needs.
- Introduce intelligent partitioning and coldstore spill for older data to reduce per-tenant storage costs.
- Surface clear cost guardrails and automatic throttles to prevent noisy tenants from impacting cluster performance.

Scope (what this ticket delivers):
1) Ingestion API change: add event priority field (system|admin|audit|debug) and tenant-cost-profile tag so producers can mark critical RBAC/SSO events. Backfill mapper to enrich existing events based on action_type and actor_role.
2) Dynamic partitioning: route events into per-tenant or tenant-group indices depending on monthly event volume thresholds. Small tenants go to shared partitioned indices; large/high-compliance tenants get dedicated indices with stronger indexing and retention.
3) Hot/cold lifecycle: implement TTL-driven rollover into coldstore with compacted index representation (column-pruned) after 30/90/365 days configurable per-tenant. Coldstore objects use compressed append-only blobs to lower storage cost.
4) Cost QoS controller: quota and burst tokens per tenant with automatic backpressure; admin escalation flow for tenants that need more capacity.
5) Query indexing strategy: selective primary indexing for security fields (actor_id, action, resource, outcome) and optional full-text for tenants that opt-in to richer search.
6) RBAC v2 integration: ensure index access respects tenant-level RBAC; SSO admin events are only visible to users with audit_read permission.
7) Observability and alerts: dashboards for per-tenant ingestion rate, storage spend, index latency, and throttling incidents.
8) Migration plan: phased cutover with canary tenants, backfill job to re-index critical historical events for tenants in dedicated indices.

Design notes:
- Use tenant fingerprint (tenant_id % N) to place small-tenant shards into stable partitions to avoid shard explosion.
- Dedicated indices for high-volume tenants should be autoscalable and pinned to dedicated node pools (Dedicated capacity or private).
- Cost QoS controller runs in the ingest path and emits trace/span with reason code when throttling is applied.
- Retention policies are owner-configurable but constrained by organization-wide max retention in compliance mode.

Acceptance criteria:
- Ingest path accepts and persists event priority and tenant-cost-profile metadata.
- Tenants above volume threshold are routed to dedicated index with correct mapping.
- Coldstore rollover reduces hot index storage for 90-day dataset by >=40% in benchmark test.
- Query SLA: admin/audit queries for recent 7 days return within 200ms P95 for dedicated-index tenants under normal load.
- Throttling events emit audit entries and are visible in console alerts.
- RBAC enforcement validated: tenant_restricted indexes are unreadable by users lacking audit_read.

Rollout plan:
- Week 1: schema change and feature flag behind "tenant-qos-audit". Unit/integration tests.
- Week 2: deploy ingest QoS controller to staging, enable for 5 non-prod tenants.
- Week 3: canary enable for 3 low-risk prod tenants; exercise backfill for last 90 days.
- Week 4: enable for top 10 heavy tenants with manual monitoring and ops on-call.
- Post-rollout: 30-day review and iterate on thresholds and cost models.

Monitoring & metrics to add:
- per_tenant_ingest_rate, per_tenant_storage_cost_estimate, throttles_by_reason, index_rollover_latency, coldstore_spill_rate, backfill_progress

Testing plan:
- Load test with synthetic tenants (1x, 10x, 100x loads) to validate partitioning and throttling behavior.
- Verify RBAC access controls by scripted user roles and SSO flows.
- Cost analysis using billing-sim harness to model monthly spend with and without coldstore.

Migration/cleanup:
- Backfill job runs in low-traffic windows and marks completed segments. Old unified indices flagged read-only after backfill and then compacted for long-term archive retention.

Notes on security_review_required: this change introduces schema and access controls touching RBAC and SSO events — schedule SecReview with compliance and cloud security teams before canary.

title: Redefine loading gesture and icon hitbox behavior to fix high-DPI regression and unify affordances
preview: Background: After the theme token migration and the recent compact-mode update, engineering shipped a patch that unintentionally reduced icon-only button hitboxes on some high-DPI displays and introduced inconsistent loading affordances between 'ghost' and 'primary' variants. This ticket defines the proposed design spec and handoff to fix the regression, unify loading/disabled gestures, and document token changes.
text: Background: After the theme token migration and the recent compact-mode update, engineering shipped a patch that unintentionally reduced icon-only button hitboxes on some high-DPI displays and introduced inconsistent loading affordances between 'ghost' and 'primary' variants. This ticket defines the proposed design spec and handoff to fix the regression, unify loading/disabled gestures, and document token changes.

title: Event envelope gating and client capability negotiation for analytics rollout
preview: Summary: This ticket implements a backwards-compatible rollout mechanism for analytics event envelopes across SDK/CLI/API clients and the ingestion pipeline. Rather than a hard-cut schema bump, we'll add a capability negotiation header + event 'envelope_version' field and gate new fields behind per-client feature flags and server-side capability checks. The goals are: (1) ensure no client-side crashes when new optional fields appear, (2) give visibility into adoption by client version, and (3) enable incremental rolling transforms in the analytics pipeline with an easy rollback path.
text: Summary: This ticket implements a backwards-compatible rollout mechanism for analytics event envelopes across SDK/CLI/API clients and the ingestion pipeline. Rather than a hard-cut schema bump, we'll add a capability negotiation header + event 'envelope_version' field and gate new fields behind per-client feature flags and server-side capability checks. The goals are: (1) ensure no client-side crashes when new optional fields appear, (2) give visibility into adoption by client version, and (3) enable incremental rolling transforms in the analytics pipeline with an easy rollback path.

title: Emergency follow-up: rate-tier misattribution during traffic-shaper rollout caused regional 429 surge
preview: Summary: During a staged rollout of the new traffic-shaper configuration (traffic-shaper v2) we observed a regional surge of 429 responses that lasted ~47 minutes across two east-coast datacenters. The canary policy incorrectly mapped several enterprise and free-tier rate-tiers to the same enforcement bucket due to a translation bug in the rollout propagation service. This caused aggressive token draining for many customers and triggered client-side retries that amplified the storm. This ticket captures the root cause analysis, mitigations applied, and the follow-up action plan to prevent recurrence.
text: Summary: During a staged rollout of the new traffic-shaper configuration (traffic-shaper v2) we observed a regional surge of 429 responses that lasted ~47 minutes across two east-coast datacenters. The canary policy incorrectly mapped several enterprise and free-tier rate-tiers to the same enforcement bucket due to a translation bug in the rollout propagation service. This caused aggressive token draining for many customers and triggered client-side retries that amplified the storm. This ticket captures the root cause analysis, mitigations applied, and the follow-up action plan to prevent recurrence.

title: Servepath Northstar: consolidated p50/p95/p99 latency panels by model, route and IO-mode
preview: Create a single, browsable dashboard (Servepath Northstar) that surfaces p50/p95/p99 latency across model and route dimensions with an explicit split between streaming and non-streaming IO-mode. This ticket covers: 1) tracing hook additions to tag spans with model_id, route_id, and io_mode; 2) a metrics pipeline update to publish aggregated latency histograms and percentiles keyed by (model, route, io_mode); 3) dashboard panels and comparison views for engineers and SREs to quickly identify regressions and capacity pressure. The goal is to reduce time-to-root-cause for latency incidents and make regression detection actionable via alerts and drilldowns.
text: Create a single, browsable dashboard (Servepath Northstar) that surfaces p50/p95/p99 latency across model and route dimensions with an explicit split between streaming and non-streaming IO-mode. This ticket covers: 1) tracing hook additions to tag spans with model_id, route_id, and io_mode; 2) a metrics pipeline update to publish aggregated latency histograms and percentiles keyed by (model, route, io_mode); 3) dashboard panels and comparison views for engineers and SREs to quickly identify regressions and capacity pressure. The goal is to reduce time-to-root-cause for latency incidents and make regression detection actionable via alerts and drilldowns.

title: Retention policy service: CRUD API, persistence, and change history
preview:
Problem
Regulated customers (Dedicated/Private) are blocked on having productized retention controls with auditable change history. We need a control-plane service to store retention policies, validate them against tier constraints, and expose CRUD + export endpoints used by (a) the evaluator, (b) purge/TTL jobs, (c) Console retention settings UI, and (d) compliance report pack generator.

Scope (this ticket)
1) Retention policy data model + persistence
- Add Postgres tables for retention policies and change history.
- Ensure the model supports: policy versioning (schema version), tenant + (optional) region scope, per-data-class retention settings, enable/disable modes for specific data classes, and metadata needed for auditability.
- Persist immutable change history records on every create/update/delete.

2) Control-plane API endpoints
- CRUD endpoints for tenant-scoped retention policy:
  - GET /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/retention-policy (read current effective policy config + metadata)
  - PUT /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/retention-policy (upsert full policy)
  - PATCH /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/retention-policy (optional; if we support partial updates in v1)
  - DELETE /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/retention-policy (revert to tier defaults; does not bypass legal holds)
- GET /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/retention-policy/history?from=...&to=... (change history)
- GET /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/retention-policy/export (stable export format for compliance pack; includes current policy + relevant metadata like updated_at/updated_by)

3) Validation + error semantics
- Enforce tier-based min/max TTL per data class (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private) with clear error messages.
- Prevent invalid combinations (e.g., setting retention for a data class that is disabled by tenant logging mode; region overrides without a base policy; TTL < minimum; unsupported schema version).
- Validate legal hold semantics: policy changes cannot shorten retention if legal hold applies to a data class/scope (exact rules per legal hold doc; this ticket should add the “policy service checks hold state” hook, even if the hold workflow implementation is tracked elsewhere).
- Provide consistent error codes for Console and Terraform:
  - INVALID_ARGUMENT (schema/TTL)
  - FAILED_PRECONDITION (legal hold active / logging disabled)
  - PERMISSION_DENIED (RBAC)
  - CONFLICT (optimistic concurrency mismatch)

4) Auditability / change history requirements
- Every mutation writes an audit log event (via existing audit logging library) AND a DB change record.
- Change history record must include: tenant_id, actor (user/service principal), request_id, timestamp, operation (CREATE/UPDATE/DELETE), previous_policy_hash, new_policy_hash, and a redacted diff summary (no secrets; no customer prompt content).
- Support optimistic concurrency (If-Match / etag) to prevent accidental overwrites from Console vs Terraform.

Non-goals
- Policy evaluation logic (separate module / ticket; see PR links).
- TTL enforcement and purge jobs in data stores (separate tickets ENG-18433/ENG-18434/etc.).
- Full legal hold workflow UI (tracked elsewhere); only integrate “hold check” at write time.

Acceptance criteria
- API supports create/read/update/delete of retention policy for a tenant in staging.
- Change history is queryable and includes actor + request id for every mutation.
- Export endpoint produces deterministic output (versioned) suitable for compliance report pack generator.
- Validation rules match the schema and tier constraints documented in Confluence; errors are actionable.
- RBAC: only Security Admin / Org Admin can mutate; read access for Auditor role (per Console spec).
- Unit tests for validation and persistence; integration test that performs upsert -> export -> history.

Implementation notes
- Data model proposal:
  - retention_policies (tenant_id PK, schema_version, policy_json, etag, updated_at, updated_by)
  - retention_policy_changes (id PK, tenant_id, occurred_at, actor_type, actor_id/email, request_id, operation, prev_hash, new_hash, diff_summary, schema_version)
- Hashing: stable canonical JSON normalization before hashing to avoid noisy diffs.
- Export format: align to ADR-018 for versioning and backwards compatibility. Prefer JSON export initially; CSV/PDF handled by report pack generator.

Rollout plan
- Gate writes behind feature flag: retention_policy_service_write_enabled (default off in prod).
- Reads can be enabled earlier for internal dogfooding.
- Backfill: when enabling, create implicit “default policy” records for existing Dedicated/Private tenants only if required by downstream systems; otherwise evaluator can fall back to tier defaults.

Links
- Confluence: Retention controls overview (data classes + defaults)
  sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/data-residency-and-retention/retention-controls-overview.json
- Confluence: Retention policy schema + validation rules
  sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/data-residency-and-retention/retention-policy-schema-and-validation.json
- ADR: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
  sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-017-retention-policy-evaluation-in-control-plane.json
- ADR: Compliance report pack export format
  sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-018-compliance-report-pack-export-format.json
- Console UX spec (permissions + change history UI expectations)
  sources/confluence/product-docs/design-specs/console-retention-settings-page-spec.json

Dependencies / follow-ons (tracked separately)
- Blocks ENG-18433 (TTL enforcement for request logs) and ENG-18434 (audit logs TTL) on “policy lookup + export endpoint stability”.
- Report generator ENG-18441 relies on /export endpoint and history query for evidence package.

Security review checklist (required before GA)
- [ ] Threat model update for policy write paths (abuse/privilege escalation)
- [ ] Confirm audit log event types/names for retention changes with SecEng (Marcus)
- [ ] Confirm no sensitive fields in diff_summary
- [ ] Validate RBAC roles match Console + Private installer expectations
- [ ] Confirm legal hold check behavior and error messaging with Legal/GRC
text:
Problem
Regulated customers (Dedicated/Private) are blocked on having productized retention controls with auditable change history. We need a control-plane service to store retention policies, validate them against tier constraints, and expose CRUD + export endpoints used by (a) the evaluator, (b) purge/TTL jobs, (c) Console retention settings UI, and (d) compliance report pack generator.

Scope (this ticket)
1) Retention policy data model + persistence
- Add Postgres tables for retention policies and change history.
- Ensure the model supports: policy versioning (schema version), tenant + (optional) region scope, per-data-class retention settings, enable/disable modes for specific data classes, and metadata needed for auditability.
- Persist immutable change history records on every create/update/delete.

2) Control-plane API endpoints
- CRUD endpoints for tenant-scoped retention policy:
  - GET /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/retention-policy (read current effective policy config + metadata)
  - PUT /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/retention-policy (upsert full policy)
  - PATCH /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/retention-policy (optional; if we support partial updates in v1)
  - DELETE /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/retention-policy (revert to tier defaults; does not bypass legal holds)
- GET /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/retention-policy/history?from=...&to=... (change history)
- GET /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/retention-policy/export (stable export format for compliance pack; includes current policy + relevant metadata like updated_at/updated_by)

3) Validation + error semantics
- Enforce tier-based min/max TTL per data class (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private) with clear error messages.
- Prevent invalid combinations (e.g., setting retention for a data class that is disabled by tenant logging mode; region overrides without a base policy; TTL < minimum; unsupported schema version).
- Validate legal hold semantics: policy changes cannot shorten retention if legal hold applies to a data class/scope (exact rules per legal hold doc; this ticket should add the “policy service checks hold state” hook, even if the hold workflow implementation is tracked elsewhere).
- Provide consistent error codes for Console and Terraform:
  - INVALID_ARGUMENT (schema/TTL)
  - FAILED_PRECONDITION (legal hold active / logging disabled)
  - PERMISSION_DENIED (RBAC)
  - CONFLICT (optimistic concurrency mismatch)

4) Auditability / change history requirements
- Every mutation writes an audit log event (via existing audit logging library) AND a DB change record.
- Change history record must include: tenant_id, actor (user/service principal), request_id, timestamp, operation (CREATE/UPDATE/DELETE), previous_policy_hash, new_policy_hash, and a redacted diff summary (no secrets; no customer prompt content).
- Support optimistic concurrency (If-Match / etag) to prevent accidental overwrites from Console vs Terraform.

Non-goals
- Policy evaluation logic (separate module / ticket; see PR links).
- TTL enforcement and purge jobs in data stores (separate tickets ENG-18433/ENG-18434/etc.).
- Full legal hold workflow UI (tracked elsewhere); only integrate “hold check” at write time.

Acceptance criteria
- API supports create/read/update/delete of retention policy for a tenant in staging.
- Change history is queryable and includes actor + request id for every mutation.
- Export endpoint produces deterministic output (versioned) suitable for compliance report pack generator.
- Validation rules match the schema and tier constraints documented in Confluence; errors are actionable.
- RBAC: only Security Admin / Org Admin can mutate; read access for Auditor role (per Console spec).
- Unit tests for validation and persistence; integration test that performs upsert -> export -> history.

Implementation notes
- Data model proposal:
  - retention_policies (tenant_id PK, schema_version, policy_json, etag, updated_at, updated_by)
  - retention_policy_changes (id PK, tenant_id, occurred_at, actor_type, actor_id/email, request_id, operation, prev_hash, new_hash, diff_summary, schema_version)
- Hashing: stable canonical JSON normalization before hashing to avoid noisy diffs.
- Export format: align to ADR-018 for versioning and backwards compatibility. Prefer JSON export initially; CSV/PDF handled by report pack generator.

Rollout plan
- Gate writes behind feature flag: retention_policy_service_write_enabled (default off in prod).
- Reads can be enabled earlier for internal dogfooding.
- Backfill: when enabling, create implicit “default policy” records for existing Dedicated/Private tenants only if required by downstream systems; otherwise evaluator can fall back to tier defaults.

Links
- Confluence: Retention controls overview (data classes + defaults)
  sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/data-residency-and-retention/retention-controls-overview.json
- Confluence: Retention policy schema + validation rules
  sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/data-residency-and-retention/retention-policy-schema-and-validation.json
- ADR: Retention policy evaluation in control plane
  sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-017-retention-policy-evaluation-in-control-plane.json
- ADR: Compliance report pack export format
  sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-018-compliance-report-pack-export-format.json
- Console UX spec (permissions + change history UI expectations)
  sources/confluence/product-docs/design-specs/console-retention-settings-page-spec.json

Dependencies / follow-ons (tracked separately)
- Blocks ENG-18433 (TTL enforcement for request logs) and ENG-18434 (audit logs TTL) on “policy lookup + export endpoint stability”.
- Report generator ENG-18441 relies on /export endpoint and history query for evidence package.

Security review checklist (required before GA)
- [ ] Threat model update for policy write paths (abuse/privilege escalation)
- [ ] Confirm audit log event types/names for retention changes with SecEng (Marcus)
- [ ] Confirm no sensitive fields in diff_summary
- [ ] Validate RBAC roles match Console + Private installer expectations
- [ ] Confirm legal hold check behavior and error messaging with Legal/GRC

title: Add generics-based Bind/Unbind helpers and StreamSink for typed streaming responses
preview:
Problem: SDK users need a concise, type-safe ergonomic surface for decoding structured responses from both single-shot and streaming endpoints. Current helpers are ad-hoc: callers implement custom unmarshal loops or rely on raw json.RawMessage plumbing. This causes duplication, brittle partial-parse handling in streaming scenarios, and poor DX for generics-based model outputs (e.g., typed tool call payloads, structured eval JSON).

Goal: Introduce a small set of composable, generics-aware helpers in the go-sdk that: 
- Provide Bind[T] / Unbind[T] helpers to map between raw bytes/values and typed structs with stable error semantics.
- Add StreamSink[T] abstraction that receives decoded typed events from a streaming decoder and exposes pause/resume, backpressure hooks, and a safe close semantics for retries.
- Preserve backward compatibility with the existing Decoder/Stream APIs and keep allocations low for common patterns.

Design notes and trade-offs: 
- API surface: prefer two thin types: Decoder[T] (for both single-shot and streaming) + StreamSink[T] (consumer-side typed event sink). Avoid adding many helpers that encourage coupling to internal JSON shapes.
- Error model: Decoder[T] returns (T, error) for single-shot; streaming decoder emits events that can be partial (e.g., incremental tokens) and terminal events. StreamSink[T] must surface both transient errors (retryable) and terminal parse failures. We introduce a ParseStatus type (as string constants) instead of an enum to keep the surface small.
- Performance: we benchmarked three approaches: reflect-based unmarshal, codegen (not in scope), and generics+encoding/json. Generics+encoding/json had ~8-12% overhead vs raw json.RawMessage unmarshalling for 1k token payloads but reduced allocation churn. We accept this trade-off for clarity; a future opt-in codegen (or use of easyjson) could be added.
- Backwards compatibility: the old Decoder interface remains intact. New helpers are additive. We provide adapters: FromLegacyDecoder[T](LegacyDecoder) to convert existing code.

Current helpers are ad-hoc: callers implement custom unmarshal loops or rely on raw json.RawMessage plumbing. - Add StreamSink[T] abstraction that receives decoded typed events from a streaming decoder and exposes pause/resume, backpressure hooks, and a safe close semantics for retries.
- Preserve backward compatibility with the existing Decoder/Stream APIs and keep allocations low for common patterns.

Design notes and trade-offs: 
- API surface: prefer two thin types: Decoder[T] (for both single-shot and streaming) + StreamSink[T] (consumer-side typed event sink). Avoid adding many helpers that encourage coupling to internal JSON shapes.
- Error model: Decoder[T] returns (T, error) for single-shot; streaming decoder emits events that can be partial (e.g., incremental tokens) and terminal events. StreamSink[T] must surface both transient errors (retryable) and terminal parse failures. We introduce a ParseStatus type (as string constants) instead of an enum to keep the surface small.
- Performance: we benchmarked three approaches: reflect-based unmarshal, codegen (not in scope), and generics+encoding/json. Generics+encoding/json had ~8-12% overhead vs raw json.RawMessage unmarshalling for 1k token payloads but reduced allocation churn. We accept this trade-off for clarity; a future opt-in codegen (or use of easyjson) could be added.

title: RBACv2 litmus-wave migration and audit holdback stabilization
preview: Goal: perform a staged migration from RBAC v1 to RBAC v2 ensuring no gaps in audit trails, predictable admin controls, and SSO/SAML mapping fidelity. We will validate dual-write, run a reconciliation pipeline, execute litmus waves (small customer cohorts), and keep a final audit holdback wave to verify continuity before global cutover. The emphasis is on observable safety gates, clear rollback hooks, and minimal customer-facing disruption.
text: Goal: perform a staged migration from RBAC v1 to RBAC v2 ensuring no gaps in audit trails, predictable admin controls, and SSO/SAML mapping fidelity. We will validate dual-write, run a reconciliation pipeline, execute litmus waves (small customer cohorts), and keep a final audit holdback wave to verify continuity before global cutover. The emphasis is on observable safety gates, clear rollback hooks, and minimal customer-facing disruption.

title: Investigate connection-pool starvation causing tenant 5xx bursts; implement staggered retries and circuit breaker
preview:
Summary: Several enterprise customers reported repeated 5xx spikes and timeouts originating from the Hosted API during concentrated tenant traffic bursts. Investigation shows a pattern of connection-pool starvation on the edge proxies combined with a retry amplification loop in clients, producing transient 502/504 errors and elevated tail latency.

Root cause hypothesis:
- HTTP/2 stream lifecycle bug in our downstream client (edge -> inference backend) occasionally leaves streams in a HALF_CLOSED state when backend returns certain error responses, preventing connection reuse.
- Per-host connection semaphore is saturated for affected tenants because leaked streams reduce available concurrency.
- Client-side aggressive retry logic (customer SDKs + internal gateway) retries immediately, amplifying the surge and preventing pool recovery.

Proposed fixes (this ticket):
1) Fix stream cleanup path in downstream client to ensure streams are always returned to the pool on any terminal state. (Code change + unit tests.)
2) Add a per-tenant concurrency guard (lightweight semaphore) in the edge path to bound active requests and fail fast when a tenant exceeds burst capacity.
3) Implement an adaptive circuit-breaker for tenants: open when recent error rate + queue depth exceed thresholds; use exponential cooldown and track reason codes so we can auto-fallback to a lower-cost compatible model when available.
4) Replace immediate retries with a staggered jittered backoff policy in the gateway and recommend SDK backoff update; add token-bucket limiter on retries to prevent amplification.
5) Temporary mitigation: increase connection pool size and reduce idle timeout to accelerate reclamation while fixes roll out.
6) Observability: add new metrics and dashboards for per-tenant active streams, leaked-stream counter, retry amplification ratio, and circuit-breaker state. Add an alert that correlates leaked-stream rate with 5xx rate.

Rollout plan:
- Merge fix for stream cleanup + unit tests to canary branch. (PR link below)
- Deploy canary to edge cluster us-west-2 with 10% of traffic for affected tenants for 2 hours. Monitor the new metrics and SLOs.
- If stable, escalate canary percentage to 50% then 100% in 1-2 hours increments.
- Deploy concurrency guard and circuit breaker behind a kill-switch feature flag to allow quick rollback.

Customer communication: drafted template to update impacted customers with timeline, root-cause summary, mitigation steps, and ETA for full fix. Include opt-in for priority rollback if customers prefer temporary capacity bump.

Artifacts and links: 
- PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/edge-client/pull/1543
- Confluence spec: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENG/Connection+Pool+Hardening+and+Adaptive+Retries
- Dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/hosted/tenant-connection-metrics
- Escalation thread: https://slack.redwood.ai/archives/C01234ABCD/p1676070000000

Acceptance criteria: 
- No stream leaks in integration tests and canary logs show leaked-stream counter stable at zero for 6 hours under synthetic tenant spikes.
- 50% reduction in retry amplification ratio and 80% reduction in tenant-specific 5xx spikes for canary traffic.
- Feature flags to disable concurrency guard and circuit-breaker allow safe rollback within 5 minutes.

Notes / trade-offs:
- Adding per-tenant guards risks rejecting traffic for bursty but legitimate workloads; balancing thresholds requires input from SRE and Product.
- Increasing pool size as short-term mitigation has cost implications; must be time-limited and communicated to Finance.

Next steps: 
- Ethan to push fix for stream cleanup (ENG-22456-1) and request expedited PR review.
- SRE (Lina Patel) to prototype circuit-breaker thresholds using historical traffic.
- PM (Aisha Khan) to approve customer comms and coordinate with sales for impacted accounts.

title: Hybrid admit controller: golden signals fusion and token-emission SLI dashboard
preview:
Goal: build a dashboard and telemetry plumbing that fuses golden signals (error rate, latency, saturation, traffic) with fine-grained token-emission latency SLIs and a visualization of the hybrid admit controller (low-latency fast-path + regional fallback). This ticket covers the instrumentation spec, the metrics schema, a Grafana dashboard prototype, and a short playbook for oncall to interpret and triage admission-related incidents.

Why: our current dashboards surface request-level p95/p99 and high-level admission counts but do not make it easy to correlate regional admission hysteresis with token emission slowdowns or KV cache miss storms. Operators need one-pane views that show when load-shedding is protecting global SLOs and when it is regressing user-visible token emission latency.

Outcomes:
- A metrics schema for the hybrid admit controller including role (fast-path|fallback), admit decision reason, admit latency, kv_cache_hit, and per-token emission_latency histogram buckets.
- A Grafana dashboard with panels that let engineers pivot between region, shard, model-variant, and priority class.
- Automated baseline calculation for token-emission SLI and an alert recipe for drift/failure modes (e.g., KV miss surge, admission spike, token tail growth).

title: Composite Signal Health Matrix and Auto-Notify for Cost/Latency Telemetry
preview: Problem: Our current alerts are noisy and siloed per-metric (tokens, p99 latency, billing delta). Customers and SREs are repeatedly paged for symptoms without actionable triage guidance. We need a backend service that synthesizes multiple telemetry streams into a single 'signal health' score per customer/route/model, surfaces probable root causes, and drives fewer, higher-quality notifications.
text: Problem: Our current alerts are noisy and siloed per-metric (tokens, p99 latency, billing delta). Customers and SREs are repeatedly paged for symptoms without actionable triage guidance. We need a backend service that synthesizes multiple telemetry streams into a single 'signal health' score per customer/route/model, surfaces probable root causes, and drives fewer, higher-quality notifications.

title: Adaptive embedding gateway: versioned onboarding, fallback routing, and storage-compatible migration
preview: Deliver an "adaptive embedding gateway" component that enables safe onboarding of new embedding models and versioned model serving for the embeddings + reranking endpoints. Goals: (1) allow rollouts with incremental traffic and automatic fallback to prior versions when quality or latency regressions appear; (2) preserve read compatibility for previously stored vectors and provide a background migration path; (3) expose metrics and automated gates (quality drift, latency SLOs, cost delta) for rollout automation. This ticket covers API surface changes, routing policy engine, storage compatibility shim, CI/eval harness, and an operator playbook for deprecations.
text: Deliver an "adaptive embedding gateway" component that enables safe onboarding of new embedding models and versioned model serving for the embeddings + reranking endpoints. Goals: (1) allow rollouts with incremental traffic and automatic fallback to prior versions when quality or latency regressions appear; (2) preserve read compatibility for previously stored vectors and provide a background migration path; (3) expose metrics and automated gates (quality drift, latency SLOs, cost delta) for rollout automation. This ticket covers API surface changes, routing policy engine, storage compatibility shim, CI/eval harness, and an operator playbook for deprecations.

title: Deprecation sweep: compat helpers, codemods, and automated changelog for SDK v1->v2
preview: Goal: execute a coordinated deprecation sweep across Python/TypeScript/Go SDKs to make v2 the default, provide engineering-safe compatibility helpers and codemods, and ship an automated changelog pipeline so customers can find breaking changes and migration examples. Scope: (1) identify all v1 surface area to be removed/alias'd; (2) implement light-weight runtime shims and opt-in compatibility packages where removal would cause large customer impact; (3) author codemods for common patterns (streaming, retries, structured output -> function-calling); (4) update docs and sample apps with side-by-side v1->v2 examples; (5) add CI migration tests and a changelog automation job that extracts API-level deprecations from source and PR titles. Deliverables: - compat-helpers packages (python redwood_compat, ts @redwood/compat, go compat module) - codemod scripts + examples (jscodeshift for TS, rope/codemod patterns for Python, go/repair tools) - migration page with runnable snippets and diffable examples covering streaming ergonomics, retry semantics, and new structured output patterns - changelog automation that parses PR labels and deprecation annotations and publishes a machine and human-readable changelog artifact (CHANGELOG_API.json + CHANGELOG.md) - rollout plan for toggling v2-as-default in hosted API and deprecation notices for Dedicated/Private nodes Risks & trade-offs: - Heavy-handed shims increase maintenance burden; we prefer opt-in compat packages rather than leaving ambiguous runtime behavior. - Codemods cannot guarantee semantics with custom user wrappers around SDKs; emphasize CLA/backup in migration docs. - Streaming retry semantics: v2's streaming backpressure model is stricter; add an examples section showing how to migrate streaming consumers to the new async iterator + reconnect pattern. Out-of-scope for this ticket: large refactors to internals (kernel selection), language-specific re-architecting of SDKs beyond surface shims. Links and artifacts: - Migration notebook (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/SDK/SDK+v1->v2+Deprecation+Sweep - Draft migration page (Drive): https://drive.redwood.ai/drive/folders/SDK-migration-drafts - Draft PRs: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-python/pull/834, https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-js/pull/1201, https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-go/pull/279 - Codemod repo: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-codemods - Changelog automation PoC: https://github.com/redwood-inference/changelog-bot (contains rule set for deprecation parsing and sample webhook) Acceptance criteria: 1) Compat packages published to PyPI/NPM/go proxy with basic test coverage and a README migration example for streaming and retry cases. 2) Codemods able to transform 80% of internal migration patterns used in our sample apps. 3) Changelog automation added to CI that emits CHANGELOG_API.json and a human CHANGELOG.md on release branches. 4) Docs updated with step-by-step migration recipes and a rollback section. 5) Runbook for customer support and SEs describing common migration gotchas and recommended fixes.
text: Goal: execute a coordinated deprecation sweep across Python/TypeScript/Go SDKs to make v2 the default, provide engineering-safe compatibility helpers and codemods, and ship an automated changelog pipeline so customers can find breaking changes and migration examples. Scope: (1) identify all v1 surface area to be removed/alias'd; (2) implement light-weight runtime shims and opt-in compatibility packages where removal would cause large customer impact; (3) author codemods for common patterns (streaming, retries, structured output -> function-calling); (4) update docs and sample apps with side-by-side v1->v2 examples; (5) add CI migration tests and a changelog automation job that extracts API-level deprecations from source and PR titles. Deliverables: - compat-helpers packages (python redwood_compat, ts @redwood/compat, go compat module) - codemod scripts + examples (jscodeshift for TS, rope/codemod patterns for Python, go/repair tools) - migration page with runnable snippets and diffable examples covering streaming ergonomics, retry semantics, and new structured output patterns - changelog automation that parses PR labels and deprecation annotations and publishes a machine and human-readable changelog artifact (CHANGELOG_API.json + CHANGELOG.md) - rollout plan for toggling v2-as-default in hosted API and deprecation notices for Dedicated/Private nodes Risks & trade-offs: - Heavy-handed shims increase maintenance burden; we prefer opt-in compat packages rather than leaving ambiguous runtime behavior. - Codemods cannot guarantee semantics with custom user wrappers around SDKs; emphasize CLA/backup in migration docs. - Streaming retry semantics: v2's streaming backpressure model is stricter; add an examples section showing how to migrate streaming consumers to the new async iterator + reconnect pattern. Out-of-scope for this ticket: large refactors to internals (kernel selection), language-specific re-architecting of SDKs beyond surface shims. Links and artifacts: - Migration notebook (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/SDK/SDK+v1->v2+Deprecation+Sweep - Draft migration page (Drive): https://drive.redwood.ai/drive/folders/SDK-migration-drafts - Draft PRs: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-python/pull/834, https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-js/pull/1201, https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-go/pull/279 - Codemod repo: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-codemods - Changelog automation PoC: https://github.com/redwood-inference/changelog-bot (contains rule set for deprecation parsing and sample webhook) Acceptance criteria: 1) Compat packages published to PyPI/NPM/go proxy with basic test coverage and a README migration example for streaming and retry cases. 2) Codemods able to transform 80% of internal migration patterns used in our sample apps. 3) Changelog automation added to CI that emits CHANGELOG_API.json and a human CHANGELOG.md on release branches. 4) Docs updated with step-by-step migration recipes and a rollback section. 5) Runbook for customer support and SEs describing common migration gotchas and recommended fixes.

title: OTLP proxy with lazy schema negotiation and tenant-priority queues for tracing ingestion
preview: Build a lightweight OTLP ingress proxy that can accept heterogeneous tracing clients (OTLP/Zipkin/Jaeger HTTP/gRPC) and route/transform spans to appropriate backend sinks (Tempo, Jaeger, internal S3 archive) with: lazy schema negotiation (detects and adapts to span field variants at runtime), per-tenant priority queues with aging to avoid starvation, and adaptive backpressure/circuit-breakers per backend. Goal is to reduce dropped spans during backend incidents, improve observability SLOs, and simplify downstream exporter logic.
text: Build a lightweight OTLP ingress proxy that can accept heterogeneous tracing clients (OTLP/Zipkin/Jaeger HTTP/gRPC) and route/transform spans to appropriate backend sinks (Tempo, Jaeger, internal S3 archive) with: lazy schema negotiation (detects and adapts to span field variants at runtime), per-tenant priority queues with aging to avoid starvation, and adaptive backpressure/circuit-breakers per backend. Goal is to reduce dropped spans during backend incidents, improve observability SLOs, and simplify downstream exporter logic.

title: admin-action-audit-bridge-with-cryptographic-timestamps-and-escrow-hooks
preview: Objective: Build an "audit bridge" adapter that captures admin actions (role/grant changes, config updates, emergency overrides), signs each event with a cryptographic timestamp, writes an append-only copy to a WORM-backed escrow store, and surfaces tamper-evidence and retention metadata for compliance teams. This ticket focuses on the engineering integration: event schema, signing envelope, escrow API, residency tagging, and export hooks for legal-hold retrieval.
text: Objective: Build an "audit bridge" adapter that captures admin actions (role/grant changes, config updates, emergency overrides), signs each event with a cryptographic timestamp, writes an append-only copy to a WORM-backed escrow store, and surfaces tamper-evidence and retention metadata for compliance teams. This ticket focuses on the engineering integration: event schema, signing envelope, escrow API, residency tagging, and export hooks for legal-hold retrieval.
